title: Pork loin with mustard cream
subtitle: Pan-fried pork loin steaks in a quick wholegrain mustard cream sauce.
excerpt: Pork loin steaks seared until golden, then finished in a sauce of double cream, wholegrain mustard, and a splash of white wine. On the table in twenty minutes. The weeknight version of a bistro classic.
sourceNotes: Pork with mustard cream is a French bistro preparation in the Norman tradition, where cream and cider (or cream and mustard) are the default finishing sauces for pan-cooked meats. The Normandy version uses crème fraîche and Dijon mustard; the version here uses double cream and wholegrain mustard, which is more accessible in British supermarkets and produces a sauce with visible mustard seeds that gives it more texture. The technique is the same in either case: deglaze the pan, add cream, add mustard.

content.text: A good pan sauce starts with a good sear. The fond (the caramelised residue on the bottom of the pan after the pork comes out) is where most of the flavour lives. The wine deglazes it and lifts it into the sauce.
Do not add the mustard before the cream or it will split. Stir it in off the heat at the very end.

items.prepNote: cut into 2 steaks, about 2 cm thick

content.text: Pat the pork steaks dry and season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt and {{black-pepper}} of pepper. Heat {{olive-oil}} of oil in a heavy frying pan over a high heat. Sear the pork for 3–4 minutes per side until golden and cooked through. Remove to a warm plate and rest while you make the sauce.

Reduce the heat to medium. Add the {{shallot}} shallots to the same pan and cook for 2 minutes. Pour in {{white-wine-dry}} of white wine and scrape up any caramelised bits from the bottom. Let it bubble for 1 minute. Add {{double-cream}} of cream and thyme if using. Simmer for 2 minutes until slightly thickened. Remove from the heat and stir in {{wholegrain-mustard}} of wholegrain mustard. Taste and adjust seasoning. Pour the sauce over the pork.

items.symptom: Sauce has split or looks grainy
items.cause: Mustard added while sauce was too hot, or cream overreduced.
items.fix: Add mustard only off the heat. If the sauce splits, add a splash of warm water and stir vigorously.
Loin steaks need only 3–4 minutes per side. Use a thermometer: 63 °C internal temperature is correct.
Return to the heat for another minute before adding the mustard. The cream will thicken quickly.

items.cause: Added to a pan that was too hot and burnt on the outside.
items.fix: After removing the pork, let the pan cool for 30 seconds before adding the shallots.

content.text: Pan-fried pork with a cream and mustard sauce is one of the most reliable weeknight French-derived preparations in British home cooking. It requires nothing that isn't in a standard fridge and cupboard, it takes twenty minutes, and the technique teaches the pan sauce principle that applies equally to chicken, veal, and fish. The Norman cooking tradition that produced it was built on the region's dairy farming: cream was cheap and plentiful, and it became the default finishing medium for almost everything cooked in butter.
